coredata.dc:title: A carbon-quantum-dot-sensitized ZnO:Ga/ZnO multijunction composite photoanode for photoelectrochemical water splitting under visible light irradiation
coredata.dc:creator: Xiao, Jingran
Hou, Xuelan
Zhao, Le
Li, Yongdan
coredata.dc:description: A carbon quantum dot (CQD)-sensitized ZnO:Ga/ZnO composite photoanode for photoelectrochemical water splitting has been designed and tested. In the composite, a ZnO array is grown on a piece of indium tin oxide glass and facilitates the essential integrity of the photoanode. An overlayer of Ga–doped ZnO (ZnO:Ga) is then hydrothermally grown on the ZnO nanorods. A type I ZnO:Ga/ZnO junction forms with a ZnO:Ga shell possessing visible light activity and a narrow bandgap. However, a highly positive onset potential exists due to the location of the conduction band (CB) of ZnO:Ga at the high end. The deposition of the CQDs on the surface of the overlayer provides a negatively shifted CB with a type II junction between ZnO:Ga and CQDs. The CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO multijunction composite photoanode exhibits a negative shift of the onset potential and a significantly increased photocurrent density, up to 1.5mAcm−2 at 1.6V vs. RHE, with good photostability under AM 1.5 G simulated light. The multijunction composite photoanode also achieves a current density of 0.16mAcm−2 under visible light at a bias of 1.0V, which is comparable to that of ZnO under AM 1.5 G simulated light. Electrochemical impedance spectra reveal that the CQD modification acts to decrease the interfacial charge transfer resistance associated with eliminating the energy barrier in the type I ZnO:Ga/ZnO junction that causes charge transfer resistance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnO nanorods
Carbon quantum dots
Nanojunctions
Photoelectrochemical water splitting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the carbon quantum dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic diagram for the fabrication of the CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO composite array.

SEM images of the (a) ZnO, (b) ZnO:Ga/ZnO, and (c) CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO arrays. (d) The TEM micrograph of a CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO rod. (e) The HRTEM micrograph of the ZnO:Ga and ZnO interface. (f) The HRTEM micrograph of the surface of the CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO array. The inset is the HRTEM image of CQD.
(a) TEM and HRTEM micrograph and (b) FT–IR spectrum of the synthesized CQDs.
High–resolution XPS spectra of C1s and O1s peaks of the (I) ZnO, (II) ZnO:Ga/ZnO, and (III) CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO samples.
(a) UV–vis diffuse reflection and (b) PL spectra of the (I) ZnO, (II) ZnO:Ga/ZnO, and (III) CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO samples.

(a) Mott–Schottky plots of the (I) ZnO, (II) ZnO:Ga/ZnO, and (III) CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO anodes measured in 0.5M Na2SO4 solution at a fixed frequency of 1kHz. (b) Mott–Schottky plots of CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO anode.
EIS Nyquist plots of the (I) ZnO, (II) ZnO:Ga/ZnO, and (III) CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO photoanodes at OCV (a) in the dark and (b) under AM 1.5 G simulated light irradiation.

(a) Polarization curves for the ZnO (I), ZnO:Ga/ZnO (II), and CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO (III) photoanodes in the dark (dash line) and under AM 1.5 G simulated sunlight irradiation. (b) Polarization curves for the ZnO (I), ZnO:Ga/ZnO (II), and CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO (III) photoanodes under λ >420nm irradiation. (c) Steady state photocurrents of the photoanodes at 1.6Vvs. RHE in light on–off cycles. (d) Long-term photocurrents of the ZnO and CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO photoanodes under AM 1.5 G simulated sunlight irradiation at 1.1Vvs. RHE.
Schematic of the band configuration and the charge separation at the interface junctions of (a) ZnO:Ga/ZnO and (b) CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO photoanode in the dark and in the light.
The calculated flat band potential (V FB), carrier density (N d), and space charge layer width (W) from Mott–Schottky plots.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The W values were calculated at an applied potential of 1.6V vs. RHE.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The fitted resistances of the ZnO, ZnO:Ga/ZnO, and CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO anodes under dark and light conditions.

head.ce:title: A carbon-quantum-dot-sensitized ZnO:Ga/ZnO multijunction composite photoanode for photoelectrochemical water splitting under visible light irradiation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Collaborative Innovation Center of Chemical Science and Engineering (Tianjin), Tianjin Key Laboratory of Applied Catalysis Science and Technology, State Key Laboratory of Chemical Engineering (Tianjin University), School of Chemical Engineering, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center of Chemical Science and Engineering (Tianjin)
Tianjin Key Laboratory of Applied Catalysis Science and Technology
State Key Laboratory of Chemical Engineering (Tianjin University)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A carbon quantum dot (CQD)-sensitized ZnO:Ga/ZnO composite photoanode for photoelectrochemical water splitting has been designed and tested. In the composite, a ZnO array is grown on a piece of indium tin oxide glass and facilitates the essential integrity of the photoanode. An overlayer of Ga–doped ZnO (ZnO:Ga) is then hydrothermally grown on the ZnO nanorods. A type I ZnO:Ga/ZnO junction forms with a ZnO:Ga shell possessing visible light activity and a narrow bandgap. However, a highly positive onset potential exists due to the location of the conduction band (CB) of ZnO:Ga at the high end. The deposition of the CQDs on the surface of the overlayer provides a negatively shifted CB with a type II junction between ZnO:Ga and CQDs. The CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO multijunction composite photoanode exhibits a negative shift of the onset potential and a significantly increased photocurrent density, up to 1.5mAcm−2 at 1.6V vs. RHE, with good photostability under AM 1.5 G simulated light. The multijunction composite photoanode also achieves a current density of 0.16mAcm−2 under visible light at a bias of 1.0V, which is comparable to that of ZnO under AM 1.5 G simulated light. Electrochemical impedance spectra reveal that the CQD modification acts to decrease the interfacial charge transfer resistance associated with eliminating the energy barrier in the type I ZnO:Ga/ZnO junction that causes charge transfer resistance.

ce:section.ce:para: Efficient utilization of solar energy has recently become a dynamic research topic due to increased concerns with the environmental crisis and energy demand [1]. A photoelectrochemical cell (PEC) has been regarded as a promising device for solar energy utilization, which converts photons into hydrogen and oxygen with two photoelectrodes in aqueous solution [2]. Semiconductors have been chosen as electrode materials and their properties are the efficiency–determining factors. Taking the photoanode as an example, an efficient one should meet such requirements as (i) adequate conduction/valence band position that satisfies water oxidization potential; (ii) narrow bandgap for wide light spectrum absorption; (iii) high stability in aqueous solution; and (iv) effective carrier separation and transport [3]. However, due to the pinning effect of the valence band (VB) with a great contribution from the O2p orbital, most oxide semiconductors suffer from a wide bandgap or a low potential of the conduction band with a narrow bandgap (CB) [4]. Meanwhile, metal chalcogenides and oxynitrides are not stable enough for application in aqueous solutions [5,6]. Since no individual material can meet all the qualifications so far, developing heterojunction structures that unify the benefits of different materials has been recognized as one of the most useful strategy for constructing photoelectrodes with superior performance [7,8]. Practically, n–n junctions with two different semiconductors are classified as type I (straddling form) and type I (staggered form), depending on the alignment of the energy bands [9]. For type II junctions, both the CB and VB of the narrower–bandgap semiconductor should be more negative than those of the counterpart, and the energy difference facilitates the separation and transfer of the photogenerated carriers, which has been the intensively reported n–n junction. For instance, a variety of type–II heterostructures, such as ZnFe2O4/TiO2 [10], g–C3N4/TiO2 [11], CdS/ZnO [12], and WO3/BiVO4 [13], have been fabricated as photoanodes. For the type I junction, the wider–bandgap semiconductor possesses a more negative CB and a more positive VB than those of the narrower–bandgap one. The thermodynamic mechanism governing type I junctions are also intriguing, since enhanced PEC performance has been achieved. For instance, a type I CdS–ZnWO4 heterojunction has been reported with improved photocurrent density [14]. Actually, dual junctions consisting of a type II junction combined with another type II junction [15], a metal–semiconductor junction [8,16], or a p–n junction [17] have been fabricated with more enhanced photocurrents. Likewise, a type I and type II dual junction also has the potential to achieve higher PEC efficiency and great research value in understanding the thermodynamic role. Unfortunately, there has been no report of such a heterostructure so far.

ZnO is one of the most important semiconductors in photocatalysis due to its nontoxic nature, low cost, and high activity [18–20]. However, because of its wide band–gap, the pristine ZnO suffers from low efficiency in light capture, which greatly obstructs its practical application [19,21]. Doping with extraneous atoms is considered one of the tools for narrowing the bandgap as well as benefitting the charge transport and the electrical properties of a metal oxide semiconductor [22–25]. In addition, an isostructural junction coupling with local doping and compositing strategies has been proved to be applicable to improving performance in the water splitting reaction, e.g., ZnO/ZnO:Cr [25], W:BiVO4/BiVO4 [26], and ZnO:Ga/ZnO [27]. In addition, modification of semiconductor photoanodes with zero-dimensional (0D) materials, i.e., quantum dots (QDs), has been another research orientation for enhancing optical absorption as well as reducing the electron–hole recombination rate [28,29]. With broadband optical absorption and superior conductivity, carbon quantum dots (CQD) have been considered promising sensitizers for PEC cells [30]. For instance, a CQDs–C3N4 composite showed an appreciable overall water-splitting efficiency [31]. In addition, CQDs/TiO2 [30], CQDs/ZnO [32], SiNW/CQD [33], and CQDs/CdS/H:ZnO [34] have shown enhanced PEC performance with reduced electron−hole recombination rates under simulated sunlight.

Here, we report a CQDs-modified ZnO:Ga/ZnO isostructural junction for the PEC water oxidation reaction. With the narrow bandgap of ZnO:Ga, the ZnO:Ga/ZnO constitutes a composite with a typical type I junction that exhibits improved PEC performance but a more positive onset potential. For the CQDs/ZnO:Ga/ZnO ternary photoanode, the CQDs, also acting as a light absorber, provide a more negative Fermi level. With a multijunction structure, the CQDs/ZnO:Ga/ZnO photoanode shows significantly improved PEC performance with a negative shift of the onset potential compared with the ZnO:Ga/ZnO photoanode. This work provides a new paradigm for designing a type I/type II combining junction. The thermodynamic roles governing the internal and interfacial charge separation/transport and electrode water oxidation/reduction reactions in the type I ZnO:Ga/ZnO junction and CQDs/ZnO:Ga/ZnO multijunction will be discussed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs were synthesized by an electrochemical etching method. Two clean graphite rods (HWRK Chem Co., 99.99%) were immersed in ultrapure water (18.4MΩcm) with a separation of 7.5cm. A static potential of 25V was applied between the two rods for several days with continuous magnetic stirring. After reaction, the product was subjected to dialysis (molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) 1000D) to obtain the CQDs. Finally, the solution of CQDs was concentrated with a rotary evaporator at 80°C and dried by lyophilization.

ZnO:Ga/ZnO nanorods were oriented grown on an ITO glass (active area of 1×1cm2) with a two–step hydrothermal synthesis procedure as reported recently [27]. Simply, the ITO substrate coated with ZnO seed was immersed in an autoclave filled with an aqueous solution of 40mM Zn(NO3)2·6H2O (Guangfu, 99.0%) and 50mM C6H12N4 (HMT, Guangfu 99.0%) and then heated at 100°C for 3h. After that, the ITO substrate coated with ZnO nanorods was immersed in an aqueous solution of 40mM Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, 40mMGa(NO3)3 (HWRK Chem Co., 99.99%), and 50mM HMT and heated at 100°C for 12h to obtain ZnO:Ga/ZnO composite nanorods. Finally, the composite was immersed in CQD solution (0.1mg ml−1) at 110°C for 5h, followed by washing and drying.

Electrochemical measurements were performed with a CHI 660B electrochemical workstation (CH Instrument, Austin, TX) to estimate the solar photocurrent of the photoanode using a three-electrode configuration with 0.5M Na2SO4 (pH 7) as the electrolyte, Ag/AgCl in saturated KCl as the reference electrode, and a Pt filament as the counter electrode. The positions of the three electrodes were kept unchanged at each scan to minimize the effects of biases. The measured current is not IR corrected. All the potentials were referenced to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) with the Nernst equation:
V
RHE
( V ) =
V
Ag / AgCl
( V ) + 0.1976 + 0.059 × pH.

The polarization curves were scanned at 20mVs−1 between 0.2 and 2.0V vs. RHE. The samples were illuminated with simulated sunlight from a 300W xenon lamp (Perfect Light, PLS–SXE–300) with a measured intensity of 100mWcm−2 using an AM 1.5 G or λ >420nm filter from the back side. Mott–Schottky (M–S) experiments were carried out at 50mVs−1 between 0.2 and 2.2V vs. Ag/AgCl under 1kHz. The carrier density (N d), flat band potential (V FB), and space charge layer width (W) of the samples at semiconductor/electrolyte interfaces (SEI) are calculated using the M–S equations,

) /
e

0

d
,
W =
[ 2 ε
ε
0
( V -
V

) /

]
1 / 2
,
where C S is the capacitance per unit area of the semiconductor, V the applied bias between the electrodes, q the unit electron charge of 1.602×10−19 C, ε0 the permittivity of vacuum of 8.854×10−14 Fcm−1, and ε the relative permittivity of 10 [35,36]. The V FB value is estimated by extrapolating the M–S plots in the dark to
P /
C
s
2
= 0
to obtain the intercept.

The electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were measured under open circuit (OC) conditions with or without solar illumination. ZSimpWin software was used to fit EIS data according to the equivalent circuits and obtain resistance values.

The morphology was observed with a field–emission scanning electron microscope (SEM; Hitachi, S–4800) and a transmission electron microscope (TEM; JEM–2100F). The fluorescence emission (PL) spectra were acquired with a Jobin–Yvon–Fluorolog spectrofluorimeter with excitation of 315nm wavelength incident light at room temperature. The FT–IR spectrum was carried out using a Perkin–Elmer FT–IR spectrometer. The UV–visible absorption spectra were obtained by the diffuse reflection method using a Perkin–Elmer L750 spectrometer. X–ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was performed with an ESCA PHI 1600 instrument using an Mg Kα X–ray source for excitation.

The synthesis procedure of the CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO composite nanorod array is schematically illustrated in Fig. 1 . First, the ZnO array was grown on an ITO substrate through a hydrothermal synthesis step. With a second hydrothermal synthesis of a ZnO:Ga layer, the ZnO:Ga/ZnO array was obtained. Finally, the CQDs were decorated on the ZnO:Ga/ZnO nanorods through a hydrothermal process for chemical combination. The SEM and TEM micrographs of the synthesized samples are given in Fig. 2 . Fig.2a displays a SEM micrograph of the pristine ZnO array, which shows an average diameter of ∼100nm and a uniform hexagonal appearance of the aligned nanorods. After the second growth of the ZnO:Ga layer, the average diameter of the rods increases to around ∼300nm, while the shapes of the rods become scalene hexagonal, as shown in Fig.2b. Deposition of the CQDs on the ZnO:Ga/ZnO surface represents uniform coverage on the surface of the rods, as given in Fig.2c. A TEM micrograph shows the outer appearance of a rod ∼1.6μm in length (Fig.2d). The HRTEM micrograph of the interface between the ZnO:Ga and ZnO phases is illustrated as Fig.2e. ZnO:Ga and ZnO give nearly the same lattice with interplanar fringes of 0.260 and 0.257nm, respectively. Shown as Fig.2f, the HRTEM image of the CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO composite rod shows that the CQDs were uniformly distributed on the surface of the ZnO:Ga phase with the crystal plane exposed, lattice spacing 0.335nm, ascribing to the (111) facet of the graphite (JCPDS 75–2078). These results confirm that the CQDs were successfully loaded onto the ZnO:Ga/ZnO surface.

As seen in Fig.3 a, the TEM images show that the synthesized CQDs give an average size of 4nm with a uniform dispersion. From the FT–IR spectrum (Fig.1b), we can see that there are absorption bands of OH and CO groups on the CQD surface. These results are consistent with those reported in the literature [30].

The XPS spectra in the three samples, ZnO, ZnO:Ga/ZnO, and CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO, are given in Fig. S1 (in the Supporting Information) and Fig. 4 . The peaks of Ga3d, C1s, O1s, and Zn2p are observed in the XPS survey (Fig. S1). Two characteristic peaks in the Zn2p region appear for the three samples at 1021.4 and 1044.5eV, corresponding to the Zn2p3/2 and 2p1/2 core levels, which are in agreement with the previous data acquired with pure ZnO NWs [8]. Ga3d shows as an asymmetrical peak centered at 21.6eV [27]. The locations of the Zn2p region in the three samples and the Ga3p region in ZnO:Ga/ZnO and CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO show no apparent changes. As shown in Fig. 4, the peak at 284.6eV that appears in the C1s XPS spectrum of all the samples corresponds to the sp2 CC coordination arising from the adventitious carbon in the laboratory environment, which is used for spectrum calibration [37]. The C1s asymmetric peak in the CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO composite reveals that three different types of carbon atoms coexist on the surface of the CQDs. The peak at around 286.2eV suggests the presence of CO, and the peak at 288.0eV is assigned to CO [29,38]. The O1s peaks in the three samples include the contribution of three convoluted peaks. In general, for pristine ZnO with a clean surface, the component at 530.0eV is attributed to the lattice oxygen (OL) of ZnO, while the peak at about 531.5eV is associated with the surface–adsorbed OH groups, and the weak peak located at 532.4eV is due to the chemisorbed oxygen (OA) [39]. After Ga doping, the peak of OL shifts from 530.0 to 530.3eV, which is due to the higher electronegativity of Ga3+ than of Zn2+ [40]. Therefore, Ga is chemically incorporated into the ZnO lattice after the formation of the ZnO:Ga out layer. Further, with CQDs modification, the substantial increased peak intensity at 532.4eV is probably due to the OC bond of the CQDs [41].

The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) light diffuse reflection and steady–state photoluminescence (PL) spectra are depicted in Fig. 5 . The pristine ZnO has an absorption edge at 380nm, indicating a bandgap of 3.26eV, while the ZnO:Ga/ZnO composite gives an absorption edge at 435nm, corresponding to a bandgap of 2.85eV. With CQDs loading, the absorption intensity increases in the whole range, but the absorption edge remains unchanged. In the PL spectra, three peaks are observed for the ZnO sample. The emission at 380nm corresponds to the recombination of the band edge excitons [42]. The green emission centered at 550nm is attributed to the radiative recombination and the red emission at 760nm is proposed to originate from the interstitial defects in ZnO [16]. After formation of the ZnO:Ga/ZnO composite, the intensities of the PL emission peaks at both 380 and 760nm decrease, while the peak at 550nm of the binary composite is almost eliminated, thus confirming the suppression of the recombination of the photoexcited electrons and holes. Moreover, coupled with ZnO:Ga hybridization and CQDs modification, the CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO composite photoanode exhibits further decreased PL emissions for the three peaks. This demonstrates that the light utilization is enhanced and the inner charge recombination is inhibited by the formation of the multijunctions.

The Mott–Schottky (M–S) plots of the samples measured in the dark are presented in Fig. 6 , which describes the derivation of the capacitance of the electrodes at the SEI [43]. Each sample gives a positive slope, suggesting that they are n–type semiconductors with V FB close to the CB. The M–S equations are used to calculate the V FB, N d, and W of a semiconductor at the SEI [44]. The results for the three samples are listed in Table 1 . The V FB values are 0.68 and 1.06V vs. RHE for ZnO and ZnO:Ga/ZnO, respectively. The more positive V FB of the ZnO:Ga/ZnO arrays than of the ZnO arrays corresponds to a more positive bottom of the CB of ZnO:Ga/ZnO than of ZnO. The CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO exhibits a much more negative V FB at −0.61V, suggesting that CQDs provide a much more negative CB potential. The N d of the ZnO and ZnO:Ga/ZnO composite are 6.8×1018 and 2.8×1019 cm−3, respectively. The N d increase is understood as the n–doping of Ga in the ZnO lattice in the conductive ZnO:Ga shell layer [23,45]. The significantly increased N d value for CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO is due to the excellent conductivity of the CQDs. At an applied potential of 1.6V, the calculated space charge layer widths are 12.5, 5.0, and 1.2nm for ZnO, ZnO:Ga/ZnO, and CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO samples, respectively. The thinner space charge layer of CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO corresponds to a stronger electric field in the surface region, indicating better charge separation and a lower possibility of charge recombination at the SEI [46].

The EIS measurements were carried out under both dark and light conditions (Fig. 7 ). The semicircles of the Nyquist plots reflect the charge transfer resistance at the SEI. The equivalent circuits are given in the insets of the corresponding EIS plots. In the dark, the equivalent circuit employed consists of a simple Randles circuit with resistance of R s and R ct. The charge transfer resistance in this case is uniquely related to the conductivity of the bulk material at the SEI. In the light, the equivalent circuits include a series of resistances. R s reflects the adhesion of the nanorods to ITO as well as the ITO sheet resistance, almost the same for all the electrodes. R trap is related to the rate of trapping holes in surface states. R ct is the charge transfer resistance from the surface states, which is associated with electron trapping [46,47]. Thus, the internal charge transfer of the electrode material and the interfacial charge transfer across the junctions are both involved in this case. The fitting resistances are listed in Table 2 . The EIS data measured in the dark (Fig.7a) are consistent with the M–S results. The R ct values decrease after the incorporation of the ZnO:Ga shell layer and further modification with CQDs. The inner charge transfer rate is proved to be in the increasing order of ZnO, ZnO:Ga, and CQDs. On the other hand, the semicircles under light conditions (Fig.7b) are much smaller than those in the dark, which is due to enhancement in the electron conductivity in the photoanodes [48]. In fact, compared with those obtained in the dark, the Nyquist plots under light condition show the inconsistent result that ZnO:Ga/ZnO gives the maximum resistances. Interestingly, both the R trap and R ct values after the CQDs modification are significantly reduced, lower than those of the pristine ZnO. The decreased R trap indicates fast charge transfer across the CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO multijunction. The extraordinary EIS results under light conditions can be attributed to the different interface charge transfer situations across the type I and type I/type II junctions.

The polarization curves of the photoanodes presented in this work with AM 1.5 G and visible light (λ >420nm) irradiations are given in Fig.8 a and b, respectively. Under AM 1.5 G irradiation, the current density of ZnO achieves 0.15mAcm−2 at an applied potential of 0.9V vs. RHE, and remains as a plateau with the increase of the applied potential up to 2.0V. By contrast, the ZnO:Ga/ZnO anode displays an onset potential of 1.0V with a positive shift from 0.5V of the ZnO sample, which is mainly caused by the more positive Fermi level of ZnO:Ga as discussed above. Along with the increment of the applied potential, the current density of ZnO:Ga/ZnO exhibits a monotonic increase. At an applied potential of 1.6V, the current density increases to 0.45mAcm−2, three times higher than that of the ZnO. As for the CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO composite, the onset potential reduces to around 0.6V, which matches that of ZnO. In addition, the photocurrent increases along with the applied potential, which is much higher than that of ZnO in the whole potential range. The CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO anode achieves a photocurrent density of 1.5mAcm−2 at 1.6V. CQDs not only enhance the photocurrent but also negatively shift the onset potential. Fig.8b displays the photocurrent comparison of the three samples under visible light irradiation. ZnO exhibits no photoresponse, while the ZnO:Ga/ZnO composite shows appreciable visible light performance. The CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO gives a much enhanced photocurrent, which increases sharply with the applied potential after 1.3V. The onset potential is similar to that of the ZnO:Ga/ZnO sample, which means that CQDs are not visible-light-sensitive. The current densities of ZnO:Ga/ZnO and CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO are 0.04 and 0.16mAcm−2 at a bias of 1.6V; both are about one order of magnitude lower than those under simulated AM 1.5 G light. Fig.8c displays the representative chopped-light current density–time curves of the three photoanodes under illumination at simulated AM 1.5 G at 1.6V. The nearly vertical rising and falling photocurrents suggest that the carriers in the photoanodes transport very quickly, due to the direct electron transfer pathways inside the materials [20]. The prolonged photocurrents of ZnO and CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO photoanodes were examined at 1.1V under AM 1.5 G illumination, and the results are shown in Fig.8d. The photocurrent of ZnO decreases significantly with the reaction time, with about 30% left of the initial value after 2000s light illumination. The poor stability of ZnO is attributed to the fact that ZnO reacts with the photoinduced holes, resulting in photocorrosion (ZnO+2h+ =Zn2+ +1/2O2) [49,50]. In contrast, the stability of the CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO photoelectrode is greatly improved, with 75% of the initial photocurrent maintained over 2000s and more than 50% over 3600s under the illumination. The improved stability provides evidence for efficiently accelerated photogenerated electron–hole pair transfer in the composite, as well as the stable existence of the CQDs.

A schematic diagram of the band configuration and the carrier separation mechanism at the interfaces and junctions of ZnO:Ga/ZnO and CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO samples is proposed and illustrated in Fig. 9 . For the ZnO:Ga/ZnO junction in Fig.9a, ZnO and ZnO:Ga are both n–type semiconductors with Fermi levels close to the CB, while ZnO:Ga has a more positive Fermi level and a narrower bandgap than ZnO. When the ZnO:Ga/ZnO n–n junction is formed and kept in the dark, the Fermi levels equilibrate by electron flow from ZnO to ZnO:Ga [14]. This results in a depletion layer on the ZnO side and an accumulation layer on ZnO:Ga side [26]. In the light, the photogenerated electrons flow from the CB of ZnO:Ga to that of ZnO, overcoming an energy barrier, which results in the increased charge transfer resistance of ZnO:Ga/ZnO illustrated in Fig.7b. In contrast, for the CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO multijunction anode shown in Fig.9b, the CQD provides a much more negative Fermi level, facilitating a larger energy level gradient between the CQD and ZnO:Ga. When the Fermi levels are to equilibrate, the electrons flow mostly from the CB of CQD to that of ZnO:Ga. The CB potential of ZnO:Ga becomes more negative; then an unimpeded energy transfer pathway is established inside the junctions. In the light, the photogenerated electrons transfer smoothly from CQD to ZnO:Ga and further to ZnO, which manifests as decreased resistance of the CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO photoanode. In addition, the VB potentials of ZnO, ZnO:Ga, and CQDs present a stepwise arrangement, which is favorable for hole transport. The facile charge transfer would naturally reduce the recombination probability, thus inducing enhanced photocurrent.

We presented a finely configured multijunction photoanode for water splitting. The vertically aligned ZnO nanorod array serves as the light absorber and the charge transfer medium. A ZnO:Ga shell was grown in situ on the ZnO core to achieve visible light sensitivity. The CQDs, also acting as light absorbers, are decorated on the surface of ZnO:Ga. Due to the positive Fermi level of ZnO:Ga, ZnO:Ga/ZnO constitutes a composite with a typical type I junction. The CQDs, decorated on the surface of ZnO:Ga, provide a more negative Fermi level and form a type II junction with ZnO:Ga. Therefore, type I and type II junctions are combined simultaneously within this composite photoanode. The onset potential of ZnO:Ga/ZnO is positively shifted compared with that of ZnO; however, the CQDs sensitization leads to a deeply reduced onset potential. The CQDs@ZnO:Ga/ZnO composite photoanode exhibits a significantly increased photocurrent density of 1.5mAcm−2 at 1.6V vs. RHE, which is 10 times and 3.3 times that of pristine ZnO and ZnO:Ga/ZnO, respectively. Though CQDs have no visible light response, the current density under visible light irradiation also shows an enhancement, which achieves 0.16mAcm−2, four times that of ZnO:Ga/ZnO. Due to the formation of a charge barrier, the type I ZnO:Ga/ZnO junction suffers from high resistance under light irradiation. In contrast, the CQDs benefit the charge transfer in the composite due to their high charge conductivity as well as their highly negative Fermi level, facilitating an energy gradient inside the junction by energy level matching. As a result, through Fermi level equilibration, CQD sensitization eliminates the charge barrier in the ZnO:Ga/ZnO type I junction.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has been supported in part by the Program of Introducing Talents to the University Disciplines under File B06006 and the Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Teams in Universities under File IRT 0641.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jcat.2016.11.028.
